text: Classifying new, unseen data is a central theme in supervised machine learning (ML) where the classification is learned from a set of labeled training samples. Here we show that we can classify new samples using a support vector machine (SVM) implemented on a quantum processor while the number of parameters required scales only logarithmically with the dimension of the samples and the number of samples in the training set, i.e., exponentially faster than the polynomial time required to classify samples classically using sampling algorithms. A key component of this "big data" quantum ML method is a nonsparse matrix exponentiation method that allows the efficient computation of the inverse of the inner-product (kernel) data matrix.
explanation: The passage reads like a polished, high-level summary with broad claims and minimal specifics; this kind of generic phrasing is common in AI-generated texts.
